does: This paper develops a reproducible, resource-efficient framework for evaluating the security vulnerabilities of medical LLMs across clinical specialties. The framework systematically assesses two classes of attacks: jailbreaking attempts including role-playing, authority impersonation, and multi-turn manipulation tactics, and privacy extraction attacks designed to elicit sensitive information. The evaluation spans multiple medical domains stratified by clinical risk levels, from high-stakes specialties like emergency medicine and psychiatry to general practice. Critically, the framework operates on consumer-grade CPU hardware using freely available models and synthetic patient data, eliminating traditional barriers of GPU clusters, commercial API licensing, and regulated health information access. This design enables broader community participation in medical AI security research while maintaining methodological rigor across diverse clinical contexts.
connects: This work responds to a significant gap in accessible medical AI security evaluation research. It builds on prior work in LLM robustness assessment and adversarial attack methodologies while addressing domain-specific concerns emerging from the deployment of medical LLMs in clinical settings. The paper extends existing jailbreaking frameworks developed for general-purpose language models into the healthcare context, where failures carry direct patient safety implications. It also connects to privacy-focused research on information extraction from trained models, adapting these attack vectors to medical information extraction scenarios. The work acknowledges that previous security benchmarks have concentrated evaluation power among well-resourced institutions, and responds to recent calls for democratized AI safety research by removing computational and data access barriers that prevent most researchers from participating in this critical evaluation work.
enables: This framework opens pathways for distributed, community-driven evaluation of medical AI security that was previously inaccessible. By establishing reproducible evaluation protocols that run on commodity hardware with public models, it enables researchers without institutional infrastructure or regulatory preapproval to contribute systematic security assessments across specialties and attack types. The work sets up future research into specialty-specific vulnerabilities and how clinical risk stratification correlates with security robustness. It also enables rapid iteration on defense mechanisms and mitigation strategies, since practitioners can now easily benchmark their own medical LLMs against standardized adversarial scenarios. Longer-term, this accessible framework could support the development of medical AI certification standards, inform clinical deployment guidelines, and establish community norms around security evaluation that match the field's patient safety responsibilities.
